meta.title: DBX-CEA-2: Retrieval, Memory, and Governed Tooling

questions.stem: A team wants every agent reply to include a short answer plus a citations array. Where should that durable instruction live for the most reliable behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instruction layer
Only in the latest user message
Inside the retrieved passages
Inside an MLflow experiment tag

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because persistent output requirements belong in the highest-priority instruction layer. **B**, **C**, and **D** are weaker controls and do not reliably govern every turn.

questions.stem: An agent prompt contains a long brand persona paragraph that does not change task quality but uses hundreds of tokens. What is the best refinement?

options.text: Move the persona text into every retrieved chunk
Keep only instructions that materially influence behavior
Replace the task instructions with chain-of-thought requests
Convert the persona into a tool schema

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because context engineering favors high-signal instructions and avoids wasting context window budget. **A**, **C**, and **D** either add noise or change the wrong part of the stack.

questions.stem: A prompt engineer is designing a reusable system prompt for an enterprise agent. Which TWO elements most directly improve consistency across many user turns? (Select TWO)

options.text: Explicit output format requirements
Concrete guardrails and allowed tool-use rules
A long fictional backstory for the assistant
Random examples that contradict the main instructions

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because format contracts and guardrails help the model behave consistently. **C** adds token cost without clear value, and **D** makes behavior less reliable by introducing conflict.

questions.stem: When are few-shot examples most useful in a system prompt?

options.text: When the task requires a consistent transformation pattern or output style
When the engineer wants to maximize prompt length regardless of cost
Only when the agent never uses retrieval
Only for image generation workloads

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because examples are most helpful when they demonstrate a repeatable pattern the model should imitate. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false restrictions or poor prompt-design choices.

questions.stem: A golden evaluation set shows a regression after a prompt change. What is the best next step?

options.text: Roll forward because the new prompt is longer
Compare the old and new prompts against the same evaluation set and inspect failure cases
Delete the failing examples from the evaluation set
Move all instructions into retrieved documents

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because prompt changes should be tested against stable evaluations so regressions can be isolated. **A**, **C**, and **D** hide the problem instead of measuring it.

questions.stem: What is the main risk of giving an agent multiple instructions that overlap but differ in wording and priority?

options.text: The context window becomes compressed automatically
The agent may receive conflicting signals and behave inconsistently
Vector Search will stop returning chunks
Unity Catalog privileges will be bypassed

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because overlapping instructions often create ambiguity about which rule should dominate. **A**, **C**, and **D** do not describe the typical prompt-design failure mode.

questions.stem: Which prompt-writing practice usually improves maintainability for a production agent?

options.text: Mixing role, constraints, and examples into one long paragraph
Separating role, task, constraints, and output format into clear sections
Changing the output schema for every request
Hiding critical rules inside retrieved context

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because structured prompts are easier to review, version, and test. **A**, **C**, and **D** make behavior harder to reason about or less stable.

questions.stem: A Databricks team wants an index that stays synchronized with a Delta table as data changes. Which Vector Search option best fits?

options.text: A delta-sync index
A notebook-scoped pandas DataFrame
A model registry alias
A temporary SQL view

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because delta-sync indexes are designed to stay aligned with source table changes. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not managed semantic retrieval indexes.

questions.stem: Why would a team use hybrid retrieval instead of dense vector similarity alone?

options.text: To combine semantic matching with keyword precision for exact terms
To avoid embeddings entirely
To disable reranking
To force every search result to come from the same document

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because hybrid retrieval can capture both conceptual similarity and exact-token relevance. **B**, **C**, and **D** misunderstand the purpose of hybrid search.

questions.stem: What is the most common trade-off when chunk sizes are made too large?

options.text: Retrieval usually becomes more precise because every passage is shorter
Retrieved chunks may include too much irrelevant text and waste tokens
Embeddings are no longer needed
The source documents cannot be refreshed

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because oversized chunks often dilute relevance and consume unnecessary context space. **A**, **C**, and **D** are not the normal consequence of chunk size choice.

questions.stem: Which TWO retrieval improvements most directly reduce the chance that a relevant passage is buried below a near-duplicate but less useful result? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply reranking after initial retrieval
Use maximal marginal relevance or similar diversity-aware ranking
Remove metadata from the index entirely
Append every document into one single chunk

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because reranking and diversity-aware selection help surface the most useful evidence. **C** weakens filtering options, and **D** damages retrieval granularity.

questions.stem: A dataset contains policy manuals and product docs. Why add metadata filters to the retrieval layer?

options.text: To constrain search to documents relevant to the user, product, or region
To replace embeddings with static prompts
To increase hallucination intentionally
To grant new Unity Catalog privileges

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because metadata filters narrow the search space to context that actually applies. **B**, **C**, and **D** confuse retrieval filtering with unrelated platform features.

questions.stem: What is the best reason to evaluate retrieval separately from final answer quality?

options.text: Retrieval quality never affects final answers
It helps isolate whether failures come from missing context or poor reasoning over retrieved context
It guarantees the same answer for every model
It removes the need for ground-truth examples

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because separate retrieval evaluation helps identify whether the problem is search recall/precision or downstream answer synthesis. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect.

questions.stem: What problem is semantic chunking designed to address compared with naive fixed-size chunks?

options.text: It groups content by meaning boundaries so chunks keep coherent ideas together
It disables vector indexing
It guarantees lower storage cost than any other method
It makes BM25 impossible to use

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because semantic chunking aims to preserve meaning and reduce awkward splits. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false claims about chunking strategies.

questions.stem: Which signal is most useful when validating that a retrieval system is missing important documents?

options.text: Low recall on queries with known relevant passages
The assistant has a short name
A larger browser window
The warehouse uses SQL

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because recall reveals when known relevant evidence is not being retrieved. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated to retrieval validation.

questions.stem: What is short-term memory for an agent conversation?

options.text: The current in-context conversation state available to the model during a turn
A permanent warehouse table shared with every application
A backup copy of the model weights
A Delta transaction log

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because short-term memory is the conversational state placed into the prompt for the current interaction. **B**, **C**, and **D** refer to different kinds of storage or platform components.

questions.stem: Why summarize earlier turns instead of keeping the full transcript forever?

options.text: To manage token budget while preserving the most important information
To increase hallucinations
To disable tool use
To remove all user preferences from the conversation

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because summarization helps compress older context while retaining the key facts needed later. **B**, **C**, and **D** describe harmful side effects, not the design goal.

questions.stem: Which TWO examples best represent long-term agent memory rather than temporary in-turn context? (Select TWO)

options.text: A stored preference profile retrieved across future sessions
A persisted record of previous task outcomes used later for personalization
The system prompt for the current request only
The last two user messages that are still inside the context window

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because long-term memory persists across sessions and can be retrieved later. **C** and **D** are part of current-turn context, not persisted memory.

questions.stem: A team stores every conversation message forever and always replays all of them to the model. What is the main downside?

options.text: It usually increases cost and latency while making the prompt harder to control
It guarantees better grounding
It removes the need for retrieval
It prevents prompt injection entirely

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because replaying everything inflates token usage and can bury the most relevant details. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not guaranteed outcomes.

questions.stem: When would importance-based memory retrieval be preferable to simple recency-only replay?

options.text: When older facts can still be critical even if they were mentioned many turns ago
When no memory store exists
Only when the conversation has one turn
When the application never needs personalization

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because importance-based retrieval preserves key facts that recency alone might discard. **B**, **C**, and **D** remove the scenario where such retrieval matters.

questions.stem: How does MLflow tracing help with memory-related debugging?

options.text: It captures execution traces so teams can inspect what context and tool calls were used
It automatically grants warehouse privileges
It replaces the need for evaluations
It encrypts prompts by default

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because traces make it easier to inspect retrieved context, prompt assembly, and tool-use decisions. **B**, **C**, and **D** describe unrelated capabilities.

questions.stem: Which memory pattern is most appropriate for storing a user's preferred output language across sessions?

options.text: Long-term semantic or profile memory
A temporary local Python variable that disappears after the turn
A one-time notebook cell result
A random embedding vector with no metadata

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because durable user preferences should persist beyond one interaction. **B**, **C**, and **D** are transient or not meaningful for preference retrieval.

questions.stem: What does the Model Context Protocol primarily standardize?

options.text: A way for models or agents to discover and call tools through a consistent interface
A Delta Lake compaction algorithm
A notebook export file format
A Spark shuffle strategy

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because MCP focuses on standardized tool and resource interaction patterns. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated platform concepts.

questions.stem: Why should tool definitions include clear input schemas?

options.text: They help the model understand valid parameters and reduce malformed calls
They eliminate the need for authentication
They guarantee the model will never hallucinate
They replace access controls

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because explicit schemas constrain the tool contract and reduce invalid invocations. **B**, **C**, and **D** overstate what schemas can do.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices improve reliability when exposing tools to an agent? (Select TWO)

options.text: Return structured errors the model can interpret
Keep tool descriptions specific about when the tool should be used
Hide parameter names so the tool appears more flexible
Make every tool perform multiple unrelated actions

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because predictable errors and clear descriptions improve tool routing and recovery. **C** and **D** make misuse more likely and contracts less stable.

questions.stem: A data team wants agents to call governed data logic rather than ad hoc SQL. Which Databricks feature best matches that goal?

options.text: Unity Catalog functions exposed as governed tools
Notebook markdown cells
Cluster-scoped environment variables
Driver logs

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Unity Catalog functions provide governed, reusable logic that agents can call through tools. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not provide the same governed execution pattern.

questions.stem: What is the main architectural benefit of separating an orchestrator agent from specialist sub-agents?

options.text: It can route tasks to narrower context and tools instead of forcing one agent to handle everything
It removes the need for prompts
It guarantees lower cost in every workload
It makes retrieval unnecessary

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because multi-agent patterns can localize tools and context to the subtask being solved. **B**, **C**, and **D** are exaggerated or false claims.

questions.stem: Which tool design choice most helps an agent recover after a failed call?

options.text: Returning error details and guidance that distinguish retryable from non-retryable failures
Removing all validation from the tool
Always returning success even when nothing happened
Forcing the user to debug raw stack traces

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because actionable error feedback lets the agent retry, repair parameters, or ask the user for missing information. **B**, **C**, and **D** degrade reliability.

questions.stem: What is the most important reason to keep tool scopes narrow?

options.text: Narrow tools are easier for the model to select and use correctly
They make access control impossible
They stop evaluations from running
They prevent structured outputs

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because narrow, well-defined tools reduce ambiguity during tool selection. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect side effects.

questions.stem: What does token budgeting mean in context engineering?

options.text: Estimating how much prompt space each component can consume without exceeding model limits
Choosing a warehouse size for SQL workloads
Compressing Delta files
Allocating cloud storage quotas

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because token budgeting manages prompt space for instructions, history, retrieval, and tool outputs. **B**, **C**, and **D** describe unrelated budgets.

questions.stem: A conversation is approaching the model's limit. What is usually the best response?

options.text: Summarize or trim lower-value context while preserving the instructions and critical facts
Add all remaining documents anyway
Delete the system prompt first
Convert the answer to an image

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because graceful compaction preserves essential signal while staying within limits. **B**, **C**, and **D** either worsen the overflow or change the wrong thing.

questions.stem: Which TWO strategies usually preserve answer quality while reducing prompt size? (Select TWO)

options.text: Summarize older resolved discussion threads
Filter retrieval to the most relevant chunks and metadata constraints
Append duplicate copies of the same policy manual
Replace all structure with a single unformatted paragraph

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because summarization and targeted retrieval reduce noise while preserving salient context. **C** and **D** usually waste tokens and hurt clarity.

questions.stem: Why might structured JSON context outperform an unstructured prose blob for some tool-heavy workflows?

options.text: It makes key fields easier for the model to locate and reason over consistently
It eliminates token usage
It prevents the need for evaluations
It guarantees semantic search quality

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because structured context can expose stable slots for identifiers, states, and tool arguments. **B**, **C**, and **D** overclaim what structure alone can do.

questions.stem: What is the main risk of trimming context only by recency with no importance checks?

options.text: A critical older constraint may be removed even though it still matters
The model gains more parameters
Vector Search stops working permanently
The application can no longer authenticate

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because recency-only pruning may drop older but still essential facts or constraints. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated to context trimming strategy.

questions.stem: A tool returns a 30,000-token raw payload, but the agent needs only five fields. What is the best design improvement?

options.text: Have the tool return or post-process into a compact structured summary with the needed fields
Increase the prompt size until the full payload fits
Ask users to read the raw payload themselves
Store the payload in the system prompt forever

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because compact tool outputs protect context budget and make downstream reasoning easier. **B**, **C**, and **D** waste tokens or reduce usability.

questions.stem: What is graceful degradation when context would overflow?

options.text: Reducing lower-priority context and informing the user or workflow when necessary
Ignoring the model limit and sending everything
Deleting all safety instructions
Returning a random answer without evidence

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because good systems shed low-value context predictably and communicate limits. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unreliable or unsafe behaviors.

questions.stem: Why tag context sources with quality or sensitivity metadata in Unity Catalog?

options.text: So retrieval and policy logic can prefer trusted sources and restrict sensitive content
So embeddings are no longer needed
So every user automatically becomes an admin
So prompt length always decreases

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because metadata supports both governance and retrieval filtering decisions. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false implications.

questions.stem: What is the best reason to evaluate an agent with and without a new context source?

options.text: To measure whether the added context actually improves outcomes instead of just increasing prompt size
To bypass access controls
To avoid storing experiments
To force every response to cite that source

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because empirical evaluation shows whether the new context source is beneficial. **B**, **C**, and **D** confuse evaluation with governance or citation policy.

questions.stem: Which TWO controls most directly reduce the risk of sensitive data being passed into prompts? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply policy checks before retrieval or tool output is inserted into context
Tag and classify sensitive assets in the metadata layer
Ask the model to ignore privacy requirements
Allow unrestricted access to all catalogs for convenience

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because prevention starts with classification and enforcement before prompt assembly. **C** and **D** increase risk rather than controlling it.

questions.stem: What does a regression test set help detect after retrieval or prompt changes?

options.text: Whether previously solved scenarios now perform worse
Whether the warehouse changed node type
Whether the UI theme is different
Whether users renamed a notebook

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because regression sets reveal whether system changes degraded known-good behaviors. **B**, **C**, and **D** are operational details, not model-quality checks.

questions.stem: Why can untrusted retrieved context amplify hallucinations?

options.text: Because the model may confidently incorporate inaccurate passages into its reasoning
Because retrieval disables citations
Because vector search changes SQL semantics
Because tool schemas are ignored

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because bad source material can steer the model toward incorrect but plausible answers. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not the core hallucination-amplification mechanism.

questions.stem: What is the most defensible way to compare two context-window strategies?

options.text: Run the same evaluation tasks and compare quality, latency, and cost metrics
Choose the longer prompt automatically
Compare only one hand-picked success case
Ask whichever engineer wrote the prompt

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because consistent evaluation across quality and efficiency metrics supports an evidence-based decision. **B**, **C**, and **D** are biased or incomplete.

questions.stem: A user asks about EU-only support policies. Which retrieval refinement is most helpful before generation?

options.text: Apply a metadata filter for region or document scope
Increase temperature
Delete citations
Force a longer answer

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because metadata filtering narrows retrieval to the region-specific policy content. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not directly improve source relevance.

questions.stem: A tool can write to production systems. What is the safest default pattern?

options.text: Require clear authorization, narrow scope, and strong validation before execution
Let any user invoke it with free-form text only
Skip logging to reduce latency
Merge it with unrelated read-only tools

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because high-impact tools need authorization, validation, and auditability. **B**, **C**, and **D** increase the chance of misuse.

questions.stem: A support agent should remember a customer's approved escalation path next month but not replay every transcript line. What is the best design?

options.text: Store the approved escalation path as durable profile memory and retrieve it when relevant
Keep the entire transcript in the system prompt forever
Disable memory completely
Rely on random similarity over unlabeled logs

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because the durable business fact should persist as targeted long-term memory, not as an ever-growing raw transcript. **B**, **C**, and **D** are less scalable or less reliable.
